did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning model for multi-class skin cancer classification from dermoscopic images. The method employs DeepLabV3+ for lesion segmentation, feature extraction using pretrained CNNs (MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, DenseNet201), ReliefF for feature selection, and kNN for classification. Results were obtained on the ISIC-2019 and PH2 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' and presents original experimental work. It proposes a novel hybrid deep learning model for skin cancer classification, details specific methods (DeepLabV3+, MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, DenseNet201, ReliefF, kNN), describes the datasets used (ISIC-2019, PH2), and reports new quantitative results (F1 score of 93.49%, accuracy of 94.42% on ISIC-2019). While it includes a related work section, its primary purpose is reporting new technical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Hybrid Convolutional Neural Network Model for the Classification of Multi-­Class Skin Cancer
journal: International Journal of Imaging Systems and Technology
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning model for multi-class skin cancer classification using dermoscopic images. The method employs DeepLabV3+ for lesion segmentation, feature extraction using pretrained CNNs (MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, DenseNet201), ReliefF for feature selection, and kNN for classification. Results were obtained on the ISIC-2019 and PH2 datasets. The model achieved an F1 score of 93.49% and an accuracy of 94.42% on the ISIC-2019 dataset, surpassing individual models. Evaluation on the PH2 dataset yielded an F1 score of 94.43% and an accuracy of 94.44%, confirming generalizability. The findings indicate that combining different CNN models achieves superior results over individual models for skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the eight diagnostic categories in the ISIC-2019 dataset used for classification. 867 images in the dataset.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin cancer diagnosis (8 categories: actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, melanoma, melanocytic nevus, squamous cell carcinoma, and vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the model classifies images into eight diagnostic categories using the kNN algorithm. It reports classification metrics including accuracy (94.42% on ISIC-2019, 94.44% on PH2), F1-score, precision, sensitivity, and specificity. The task is multi-class classification of dermoscopic images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions used for multi-class classification. The study utilized this dataset for both training (after augmentation) and testing the proposed hybrid model.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid model after data augmentation increased the total image count to 74,022.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the final model performance (accuracy 94.42%, F1 score 93.49%).
use.other: The paper notes that the official training/test splits are not publicly separated, so test sets may vary across studies, but this study used the dataset for its evaluation.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopic images with manually segmented masks created by specialists. It was used specifically for training the DeepLabV3+ lesion segmentation network.

use.training: 80% of the dataset (2075 images) was used for training the segmentation model.
use.testing: 20% of the dataset (519 images) was used for testing the segmentation model.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A public dataset consisting of dermoscopic images used for a second evaluation to confirm the generalizability of the proposed model.

use.testing: Used for external evaluation of the model, yielding an accuracy of 94.44% and F1 score of 94.43%.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To organize and present the effectiveness of the classification method, providing a summary of true positives, false positives, false negatives, and true negatives for each class.
To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the proposed model for each skin cancer class and calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC) values.

To compare the performance of the proposed method with state-of-the-art methods and individual CNN models (MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, DenseNet201) using accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score) and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

ISIC-2019.notes: The proposed hybrid model achieved an F1 score of 93.49% and accuracy of 94.42% on the ISIC-2019 dataset. It outperformed individual models (EfficientNetB0, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2) and ablation studies confirmed the importance of data augmentation and lesion segmentation. The model achieved the highest performance for VASC and NV categories.

PH2.notes: The model was evaluated on the PH2 dataset to confirm generalizability, yielding an accuracy of 94.44% and an F1 score of 94.43%. The dataset contains 200 images: 80 Melanocytic nevus (NV), 80 Atypical nevus (AN), and 40 Melanoma (MEL).

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (DeepLabV3+ for segmentation, MobileNetV2/EfficientNetB0/DenseNet201 for feature extraction, ReliefF for feature selection, kNN for classification)
architecture_used.pretraining: MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, and DenseNet201 were pretrained models (specifically EfficientNetB0 trained on ImageNet). DeepLabV3+ was trained on the ISIC-2018 dataset.

architecture_details.feature_fusion: Concatenation of feature vectors from the three CNNs to form a 1 × 3000 feature vector
architecture_details.feature_selection: ReliefF algorithm to reduce the 1 × 3000 vector to a 1 × 1000 vector
architecture_details.classifier: k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) with default parameter values

training_details.hardware: 12th-generation Intel Core i7-12650H processor @ 4.7 GHz, 8 GB GPU, 32 GB RAM
training_details.augmentation: Random rotation and zoom operations on the ISIC-2019 dataset

preprocessing_techniques: Lesion segmentation using DeepLabV3+ to isolate skin lesions from dermoscopic images
Background replacement with black color to eliminate irrelevant background information, hairs, and air bubbles after segmentation
Image resizing to 224 × 224 × 3 pixels for input into feature extraction models
Data augmentation via random rotation and zoom operations on the ISIC-2019 dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated clockwise or counterclockwise by a randomly selected angle between -90° and +90°.
Images were generated with random zoom values in the range of [1.2, 2.0].

conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning model, which combines DeepLabV3+ for segmentation, feature extraction from MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0, and DenseNet201, ReliefF for feature selection, and kNN for classification, achieved an F1 score of 93.49% and an accuracy of 94.42% on the ISIC-2019 dataset.
The hybrid model outperformed individual CNN models, surpassing the best-performing individual model (EfficientNetB0) by 1.20% in accuracy.
Evaluation on the external PH2 dataset yielded an F1 score of 94.43% and an accuracy of 94.44%, confirming the model's generalizability to unseen data.
Combining different CNN models for feature extraction achieves superior results compared to using individual models for multi-class skin cancer classification.

Lesion segmentation using DeepLabV3+ significantly improves classification accuracy by removing artifacts such as hair, bubbles, and ink marks, with the proposed approach achieving 94.42% accuracy with segmentation versus 88.10% without it.
Data augmentation (random rotation and zoom) plays a significant role in enhancing model performance, with accuracy rates improving substantially after its application.
The ReliefF feature selection algorithm was effective in reducing the feature vector size from 3000 to 1000 while maximizing classification accuracy.
The proposed model demonstrated the highest performance for Vascular Lesion (VASC) and Melanocytic Nevus (NV) categories, correctly classifying all 218 VASC cases in the test set.
The study indicates the potential of the proposed model as an expedient, accurate, and valuable tool for early skin cancer detection.
important_remarks: The study relies on public datasets (ISIC-2019 and PH2) which may not fully represent real-world clinical diversity or demographic biases.

The ISIC-2019 dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with Melanocytic nevus (12,875 images) vastly outnumbering Vascular lesion (253 images) and Dermatofibroma (239 images), potentially biasing model performance toward majority classes.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance comparisons rely on descriptive statistics (accuracy, F1-score) without significance testing.

The segmentation model (DeepLabV3+) was trained on the ISIC-2018 dataset, while classification was evaluated on ISIC-2019 and PH2; potential domain shift between segmentation and classification datasets is not explicitly addressed.
The PH2 dataset used for external validation is small (200 images) and limited to three classes (NV, AN, MEL), limiting the assessment of generalizability across the full spectrum of skin lesions.

The model achieves very high specificity (>98%) but lower sensitivity for Melanoma (95.00%) and Actinic Keratosis (87.64%), which is critical for clinical safety as missed diagnoses can have severe consequences.
Data augmentation (random rotation and zoom) was applied only to the training set; the impact of augmentation on test set robustness or overfitting is not deeply analyzed beyond accuracy gains.

The study uses a hybrid approach combining multiple CNNs and ReliefF feature selection, which increases computational complexity and inference time compared to single-model approaches, potentially hindering real-time deployment.
The paper does not report confidence intervals for the reported metrics, limiting the assessment of result stability.
The model was implemented in MATLAB R2023b on specific hardware (Intel Core i7, 8GB GPU), which may not reflect performance on standard clinical hardware or mobile devices.
